feed.title: M Ihsan Al Hafiz
feed.subtitle: I am a PhD student at KTH Royal Institute of Technology.

entry.title: play with always_comb and always_ff in systemverilog

link.title: play with always_comb and always_ff in systemverilog

entry.content:
When we write HDL in SystemVerilog, one common best practice is to separate the value that is stored in a flip-flop from the value that will be stored on the next clock edge. A popular naming convention is to use _q for the registered value and _d for the next value.

This style is often used in a Finite State Machine (FSM). The state movement is calculated in always_comb using blocking assignment =, and the result is assigned to the _d signal. Then, in always_ff, the _q signal is updated from _d using non-blocking assignment <=.

In short:

  • _q means the current registered value.
  • _d means the next value that will be loaded into the register.
  • always_comb describes combinational logic.
  • always_ff describes sequential logic.

Why use _d and _q?

The _d and _q convention makes the design easier to read because it follows the behavior of a D flip-flop:

  • D is the input of the flip-flop.
  • Q is the output of the flip-flop.

For an FSM, the current state is stored in state_q, and the next state is calculated in state_d.

Example: simple traffic light FSM

The example below has three states: red, green, and yellow. On every clock cycle, the FSM moves to the next state.

module traffic_light_fsm (
    input  logic clk_i,
    input  logic rst_ni,
    output logic red_o,
    output logic yellow_o,
    output logic green_o
);

    typedef enum logic [1:0] {
        RED,
        GREEN,
        YELLOW
    } state_e;

    state_e state_d;
    state_e state_q;

    // Combinational logic: decide the next state.
    always_comb begin
        state_d = state_q;

        case (state_q)
            RED: begin
                state_d = GREEN;
            end

            GREEN: begin
                state_d = YELLOW;
            end

            YELLOW: begin
                state_d = RED;
            end

            default: begin
                state_d = RED;
            end
        endcase
    end

    // Sequential logic: update the current state on the clock edge.
    always_ff @(posedge clk_i or negedge rst_ni) begin
        if (!rst_ni) begin
            state_q <= RED;
        end else begin
            state_q <= state_d;
        end
    end

    // Output logic based on the current state.
    always_comb begin
        red_o    = 1'b0;
        yellow_o = 1'b0;
        green_o  = 1'b0;

        case (state_q)
            RED: begin
                red_o = 1'b1;
            end

            GREEN: begin
                green_o = 1'b1;
            end

            YELLOW: begin
                yellow_o = 1'b1;
            end

            default: begin
                red_o = 1'b1;
            end
        endcase
    end

endmodule

Important pattern

The most important part is this pair:

always_comb begin
    state_d = state_q;

    case (state_q)
        RED:    state_d = GREEN;
        GREEN:  state_d = YELLOW;
        YELLOW: state_d = RED;
        default: state_d = RED;
    endcase
end

always_ff @(posedge clk_i or negedge rst_ni) begin
    if (!rst_ni) begin
        state_q <= RED;
    end else begin
        state_q <= state_d;
    end
end

The always_comb block decides what should happen next. The always_ff block stores that decision on the clock edge.

Blocking vs non-blocking assignment

Inside always_comb, use blocking assignment:

state_d = GREEN;

This is because combinational logic should behave like immediate logic calculation.

Inside always_ff, use non-blocking assignment:

state_q <= state_d;

This is because sequential logic updates registers together on the clock edge.

Common mistake

A common mistake is to update the current state directly in always_comb:

always_comb begin
    state_q = GREEN; // Avoid this
end

This is not the correct style because state_q should be controlled only by the flip-flop in always_ff. The combinational block should calculate state_d, and the sequential block should update state_q.

Conclusion

Using _d and _q helps separate combinational logic from sequential logic. The _d signal represents the next value, while the _q signal represents the registered value. This makes an FSM easier to understand, easier to debug, and safer to modify later.
entry.summary: how to manage _d and _q in systemverilog

entry.title: Precision in PCB Layout: The Ultimate Checklist for Flawless PCB Routing Review

link.title: Precision in PCB Layout: The Ultimate Checklist for Flawless PCB Routing Review

entry.content:
Introduction

In the intricate world of PCB (Printed Circuit Board) design, meticulous attention to detail is not just beneficial; it’s essential. The process of reviewing PCB routing is a critical step in ensuring the integrity and functionality of the final product. This article provides a detailed PCB routing review checklist tailored to guide engineers through this complex task. Drawing from extensive personal experience and expertise, the checklist is designed to be comprehensive, clear, and easily understandable, offering step-by-step guidance to optimize PCB routing.

PCB Routing Review Checklist

  1. Stack Layer Check: Reassess the stack layer configuration of the PCB and consult manufacturer recommendations if necessary. This step ensures optimal layer arrangement for your specific design.
  2. DRC (Design Rule Check) Compliance: Verify that your design adheres to the DRC and aligns with the manufacturing capabilities. This ensures that your PCB is manufacturable and meets industry standards.
  3. Component Placement According to Schematic Grouping: Place components on the PCB following the grouping established in the schematic. This organization facilitates a logical and efficient layout.
  4. Power Supply Design Review: Reevaluate the power supply design to ensure stability and efficiency. This check is crucial for the overall performance of the PCB.
  5. Decoupling Capacitors Positioning: Place decoupling capacitors close to the input pads for optimal performance. This minimizes noise and stabilizes the power supply to the ICs.
  6. ESD Protection for Inputs: Ensure inputs have ESD (Electrostatic Discharge) diodes and capacitors, followed by IC converters. This arrangement safeguards the components from static damage.
  7. Avoiding Signal Stubs in Connections: Ensure no signal stubs are introduced when connecting capacitors or other components, as they can cause signal integrity issues.
  8. Component Routing for Easy Soldering: Route components at 180 degrees for easier soldering. This orientation helps in reducing manufacturing errors.
  9. Ground Signal Via Placement: Place at least one or two vias near the pad for every ground signal to ensure a solid ground connection.
  10. Power Supply Converter IC Loop Attention: Pay special attention to the loop input and output for power supply converter ICs to ensure efficient power management.
  11. Ground Vias on IC Converter Loops: Add sufficient ground vias on loops of IC converters to maintain stable operation and heat dissipation.
  12. Trace Width for Ground-in Power Supply: Use a trace width of 0.4–0.6 for ground-in power supply designs for optimal performance.
  13. Via Dimension and Finishing: Pay attention to the dimensions and finishing of vias to ensure reliability and to prevent connection issues.
  14. Ground Via under Thermal Ground Plane IC: Place ground vias under the thermal ground plane of ICs to enhance heat dissipation.
  15. Test and Debug Point Addition: Add test points (0.9 mm) and debug points (0.6 mm) to facilitate testing and debugging of the PCB.
  16. Avoiding Signals under Top Layer Metal Components: Do not route signals under metal components on the top layer to prevent interference and signal integrity issues.
  17. Layer Numbering: Mark each layer with a number sign for easy identification during the manufacturing and assembly process.
  18. Signal Plane Integrity: Avoid breaking the signal plane, as this can lead to signal integrity issues.
  19. Via and Test Point Alignment: Align vias and test points with the trace to avoid introducing stubs, which can affect signal quality.
  20. High-Speed Design Check: Review the design for high-speed signal integrity, ensuring that all high-speed traces are optimized for performance.
  21. Impedance Matching Trace Width Calculation: Calculate the width of traces to match the impedance, particularly for high-speed and RF signals.
  22. Analog Signal review: Check analog signals for integrity and noise immunity.
  23. Ground Pour Between Analog and High-Speed Traces: Implement ground pours between analog and high-speed or differential traces to reduce interference.
  24. Differential and High-Speed Trace Protection: Ensure protection for differential, high-speed, and analog traces to maintain signal integrity.
  25. Signal Differential Pair Proximity: Keep differential pairs (like USB) close together to minimize cross-talk and interference.
  26. Annular Ring Check: Ensure annular rings are sized to accommodate imprecise and inaccurate drilling, enhancing the durability and reliability of vias.
  27. Tear Drop Implementation: Use tear drops on trace-to-via transitions if necessary to reduce stress and prevent trace lifting.
  28. Ground Array Placement in Empty Areas: Utilize empty areas on the PCB by placing ground arrays, enhancing the board’s overall stability and performance.

Conclusion

The PCB routing review checklist serves as a crucial tool for engineers, guiding them through the meticulous process of ensuring a well-designed and functional PCB. By adhering to this checklist, you can significantly enhance the quality and reliability of your PCB designs, leading to efficient manufacturing.

entry.title: Mastering PCB Design: A Comprehensive Schematic Review Checklist for Flawless Circuit Creation

link.title: Mastering PCB Design: A Comprehensive Schematic Review Checklist for Flawless Circuit Creation

entry.content:
Introduction

In the world of embedded systems engineering, creating a Printed Circuit Board (PCB) is a critical and intricate process. As an engineer, ensuring the accuracy and functionality of a PCB design is paramount. This article provides a comprehensive PCB schematic review checklist based on personal knowledge and experiences. The aim is to offer clear, detailed guidance to enhance the quality and reliability of PCB designs. Whether you’re a seasoned engineer or a novice in the field, this checklist serves as an invaluable tool to refine your PCB schematic.

Schematic Review Checklist

  1. Pad Size and Datasheet Review: Ensure that the pad sizes match the specifications in the component datasheets. This verification is crucial for avoiding soldering issues and ensuring component compatibility.
  2. Use of Buses and Labels for Tidiness: Implement buses and labels to organize the schematic. This practice not only makes the schematic more readable but also simplifies troubleshooting and revisions.
  3. Grouping Components Based on Functionality: Organize components into groups based on their function. This method enhances the clarity of the schematic and aids in understanding the circuit’s overall functionality.
  4. Component Naming Convention (ABCn): (A is the component letter, ex R for resistor), (B is the number of page schematic), (C for the number of groups of the component), (n is the number of similar component in the group). This uniformity aids in easy identification and reference.
  5. Datasheet Management: Collect and organize datasheets for all components in one folder. Highlight critical information within each datasheet for quick reference. This centralized approach saves time and reduces the risk of overlooking key specifications.
  6. Attention to Electronic Parameters: Closely review voltage, current, and other electronic parameters for each component as specified in the datasheet. This scrutiny ensures component compatibility and circuit reliability.
  7. Avoiding 4-Wire Crossings: Minimize four-way wire intersections in the schematic. This practice reduces confusion and potential errors in the layout phase.
  8. Signal Pulling Considerations: Identify and implement necessary pull signals for certain lines. This ensures signal integrity and the proper functioning of the circuit.
  9. Power Capacity of Resistors: Pay close attention to the power rating of resistors, especially in LED circuits. This ensures that components can handle the expected power load without failure.
  10. Current to LED and Candela Implications: Calculate the current flowing through LEDs and understand its implications on the LED’s brightness (measured in candela). This ensures that the LEDs function as intended in terms of brightness and power consumption.
  11. Checking for Dot or Unconnected Wires: Verify that all connections are intentional and that there are no accidental disconnections or unconnected wires.
  12. Review of Power IC Converters: Thoroughly check power IC converters for compatibility and efficiency. This review is crucial for stable power supply and circuit efficiency.
  13. Inclusion of Test Points: Incorporate test points in the schematic for ease of debugging and testing. This facilitates maintenance and troubleshooting post-manufacturing.
  14. Inclusion of Mechanical Holes for Screws: Account for mechanical aspects like screw holes in the schematic. This integration aids in the physical assembly of the PCB.
  15. Detailed Design Information: Include comprehensive details for all design aspects, such as power supply parameters, LED specifications, etc. This detail ensures that all design considerations are transparent and traceable.
  16. DRC (Design Rule Check) Review: Perform a final DRC to identify and rectify any potential design rule violations. This check is vital for ensuring the manufacturability of the PCB.
  17. Connection Verification Based on Requirements: Ensure that all connections meet the specified requirements. This final check guarantees that the design aligns with the intended functionality.

Conclusion

A thorough schematic review is a vital step in PCB design, ensuring that the final product is both functional and reliable. By following this detailed checklist, engineers can significantly enhance the quality of their PCB designs. This process, while meticulous, ultimately leads to efficient, error-free production and a robust end product. Remember, the key to a successful PCB design lies in attention to detail and adherence to best practices.

entry.title: How to solve Vivado Crash on Ubuntu while running

link.title: How to solve Vivado Crash on Ubuntu while running

entry.content:
I used Vivado 2022.1 and Ubuntu 20.04. Sometimes when I run synthesis or implementation or other processes, the Vivado will stop running and crash with log in terminal like below

8160 Killed “$RDI_PROG” “$@”

To solve that problem, I searched in the forum and found one of the solutions is to check the swap memory configuration. I checked mine and found out that the default swap memory on my Ubuntu was 2G. Check the swap memory with ‘free -m’ command. I think that was too small

I changed my swap memory size with this line of command

  • enter in admin mode: sudo -s
  • Turn off all running swap processes: swapoff -a
  • Resize swap fallocate -l 1G /swapfile (change 1G to the gigabyte size you want it to be)
  • CHMOD swap: chmod 600 /swapfile
  • Make the file usable as a swap mkswap /swapfile
  • Active the swap file swapon /swapfile
  • exit admin mode: exit
  • check the swap memory size: free -m

After I made the swap memory on Ubuntu bigger, I solved the problem with a crash. Try to check the swap memory while running the process in Vivado. If you face the crash maybe the swap memory is too small.

Reference:
https://ploi.io/documentation/server/change-swap-size-in-ubuntu
